VcPass_explanation: The target 'had done' is active voice (subject 'he' is the agent performing the action), not passive voice; passive voice requires the form 'have/had + been + past participle' with the subject as semantic patient.

FullText: Then the devil said : » I will give all this authority and glory to you , for it has been turned over to me . I may give it to whomever I will .
ASTrns_explanation: The clause is morphologically passive ('has been turned over'), and the lexical verb 'turn over' licenses both an external argument (agent, unexpressed) and an internal argument (patient 'it'), satisfying the passive construction rule.
ASUncc_explanation: The clause is morphologically passive ('has been turned over'), and the lexical verb 'turn over' licenses an external argument (an implicit agent who performed the turning over), which triggers the set_to_0_if condition for passive clauses.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'has been turned over' is passive and licenses an internal argument (the implied theme 'it'), making it transitive; additionally, the construction denotes a change-of-state event where the subject functions as a patient/theme rather than an external agent.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'turned over' denotes a punctual change-of-state or boundary transition (movement from one possessor to another) without a durative process phase, matching the achievement category.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'turned over' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (transfer of possession/authority) that can be modified by a specific time-point adverbial and does not permit interruption of an ongoing process phase.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'has been turned over' denotes a completed transfer with a defined result state (authority now possessed by the devil), entailing a specific endpoint rather than an ongoing dynamic activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has been turned over' entails a transition into a resulting state (authority being in the devil's possession), which disqualifies it from semelfactive status.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'has been turned over' is morphologically marked as progressive perfect aspect ('has been'), which entails dynamic eventive unfolding and is incompatible with stative readings per CRI set_to_0_if rule 2.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'it has been turned over to me' presents a situation as actually occurring in the actual world (the devil claims authority has been given to him), without any counterfactual marking, contrary-to-fact condition, or indication that the event did not occur.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has been turned over' is not negated; the negator 'not' does not appear in the sentence, and no negative quantifiers or 'never' modify the target gram.
DtDTAM_explanation: The target clause 'has been turned over' contains no lexical past time adverb, phrasal past time adverb, or clausal past time anchor; the initial 'Then' is a logical connector (introducing the devil's statement) rather than a temporal anchor for this specific clause.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'has been turned over' entails a change of possession/control (authority and glory transferred to the devil), and that resulting state of possession holds at the reference time, matching the set_to_0_if criterion.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'has been turned over' describes a change of possession (authority and glory transferred to the devil) and the clause entails that this resulting state of possession currently holds at the reference time, satisfying the set_to_1_if criteria.
DtUniv_explanation: The verb 'has been turned over' denotes a transition into a new state (authority being transferred to the devil) rather than a situation holding continuously over time, matching the negative criterion for state-change verbs.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'it has been turned over to me' uses the passive form 'have been' + past participle and asserts a bounded transition event (the transfer of authority), presenting it as a completed whole rather than an ongoing process.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has been turned over' asserts a realized state of affairs in the actual world (the authority and glory have actually been transferred to the devil), with no modal, conditional, future, or wish context applying.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has been turned over' includes 'been' followed by a past participle 'turned over', which matches the progressive-like construction criterion for setting to 1, though technically this is a passive voice construction with the present perfect auxiliary 'has' and the copula 'been'.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has been turned over' instantiates the form auxiliary 'have' + 'been' + past participle of transitive verb 'turn', with grammatical subject 'it' corresponding to the semantic patient (the entity affected), satisfying VcPass criteria.

FullText: When the devil had completed every temptation he departed from him for a season .
ASTrns_explanation: The target verb 'had completed' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('the devil') and an overt direct object ('every temptation'), satisfying the first set_to_1_if condition.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'completed' licenses an external argument (the devil as agent) and introduces a direct object (every temptation), satisfying the set_to_0_if conditions.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'completed' licenses and realizes an internal argument (direct object 'every temptation'), so it does not instantiate ASUnrg.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'complete' denotes a durative process directed toward a defined endpoint (finishing all temptations), satisfying the first CRI criterion for accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'completed' denotes a punctual transition into a new result state (completion of all temptations), can be modified by specific time-point adverbials, does not permit interruption of an ongoing process, and lacks measurable internal duration, satisfying multiple criteria for achievement aspect.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'completed' denotes a bounded, telic situation with an inherent endpoint (finishing every temptation), naturally modified by 'in X time' rather than 'for X time', and lacks the homogeneous, durative character required for activity classification.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'completed every temptation' entails a transition into a resulting state (the temptations are now finished), which disqualifies it from being a semelfactive and marks it as a telic accomplishment.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'had completed' denotes a bounded change-of-state event with internal eventive unfolding and a clear endpoint, marking the transition from an incomplete to a completed state, which is incompatible with stative aspect.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'had completed every temptation' expresses temporal anteriority within a narrative sequence (the devil completed temptations before departing), presenting an actual event without any counterfactual marking or indication that the situation is contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'had completed' is not negated; there is no negator ('not', 'n't', 'never') modifying it, no negative quantifier in subject or object position, and no 'neither...nor' construction—the sentence is entirely positive.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause contains a clausal past time anchor introduced by the temporal conjunction 'when', which establishes a specific temporal relationship for the past perfect verb 'had completed'.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'had completed' entails a change of state (the temptation sequence transitions from ongoing to finished), and this resulting state is relevant for the subsequent narrative event ('he departed'), making it non-experiential per the edge case rule on narrative progression.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'had completed' describes a change of state (transition from incomplete to complete temptations), and the temporal clause structure 'When he had completed...' indicates that the completion was achieved and held at the reference time before the subsequent action (departure) occurred.
DtUniv_explanation: The verb 'had completed' denotes a discrete bounded event (finishing all temptations) with focus on the transition/result, not a situation holding continuously over time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'had completed' is a transition event (completion of temptation) construed as a single bounded occurrence with a clear endpoint, presenting the situation as a discrete bounded event rather than ongoing or habitual.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had completed' asserts a realized event in the actual world within a temporal narrative sequence (when X happened, then Y happened), with no modal, conditional, counterfactual, or hypothetical marking.
TLTS_explanation: The auxiliary 'had' is morphologically past, making this a past perfect construction that satisfies the first set_to_1_if condition.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had completed' is active voice (subject 'devil' is the agent/doer), not passive voice; it lacks the required 'been' + past participle structure and the grammatical object 'every temptation' is the semantic patient, not the subject.

FullText: » The Spirit of Jehovah is upon me . He anointed me to preach good news to the poor . He has sent me to proclaim release to the captives , and recovering of sight to the blind . To set at liberty those who are bruised .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has sent' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('He') and an overt direct object ('me'), satisfying the first set_to_1_if condition.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'has sent' licenses an external argument (the implicit agent 'He') and introduces an internal argument ('me'), making it transitive and not unaccusative.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'has sent' licenses an internal argument (the direct object 'me'), which satisfies the set_to_0_if condition that the target verb licenses or realizes an internal argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'has sent' denotes a punctual boundary transition (the act of sending as a single event) rather than a durative process directed toward a defined goal, and cannot be naturally modified by 'in X time' to indicate completion of a bounded durative event.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'has sent' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (the agent being dispatched on a mission) that can be modified by a specific time-point adverbial and does not permit interruption of an ongoing process phase.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'has sent' denotes a completed action with an inherent endpoint (sending me to proclaim), entailing a specific result state, and naturally takes 'in X time' modification rather than durative 'for X time', making it a bounded accomplishment rather than an activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has sent me' entails a resulting change of state (transition from not-sent to sent) and is construed as a completed action with lasting consequences, not a single punctual occurrence without change of state.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'has sent' denotes a bounded dynamic event (change-of-state: from not-sent to sent) involving internal eventive unfolding and marks a completed action, not a static condition or mental state.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'He has sent me' presents the situation as actually occurring in the actual world (the Spirit has genuinely sent the speaker), without any indication of counterfactuality, contrary-to-fact condition, or unfulfilled wish.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has sent' is not negated; no negator ('not', 'n't', 'never') directly modifies it, and no negative quantifier negates the clause containing it.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'He has sent me to proclaim release to the captives' contains no lexical past time adverb, phrasal past time adverb, or clausal past time anchor; therefore DtDTAM is not instantiated.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'has sent' entails a change of state or location (the subject being sent on a mission) where the resulting state (being in the role of messenger/proclaimer) holds at the reference time, as evidenced by the infinitive clauses that follow describing the ongoing purpose of this sending.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'has sent' describes a change of status (being sent on a mission) where the undergoer (me) is currently in that state of being sent/commissioned to perform the actions that follow (preach, proclaim, set at liberty).
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'He has sent me to proclaim release to the captives' describes a discrete bounded event (the act of sending) with focus on the transition into a mission state, rather than a situation holding continuously across a time span.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'has sent' is a transition event (sending is a punctual action with a construed endpoint) presented as a completed whole in a narrative sequence describing the Spirit's actions, meeting the criteria for perfective aspect.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has sent' asserts a realized action in the actual world within a narrative of divine commission, with no modal, conditional, future, or hypothetical marking present.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has sent' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by the past participle 'sent', with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the set_to_0_if condition.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has sent' is active voice (auxiliary 'have' + past participle of transitive verb 'send'), where the grammatical subject 'He' is the semantic agent (doer) and the object 'me' is the semantic patient, not passive voice which requires 'have been' + past participle.

FullText: » What have we to do with you Jesus , you Nazarene ? Have you come to destroy us ? I know who you are . You are the Holy One of God ! «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'come' does not introduce an overt internal argument (direct or indirect object); it is an intransitive verb used without an affected patient.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'come' denotes directed motion/change-of-location where the sole argument 'you' undergoes displacement with no external argument licensed, matching the unaccusative criterion for motion verbs.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'come' denotes change-of-location with the subject functioning as an internal argument (theme/patient undergoing movement) rather than an external argument, matching the set_to_0_if criterion for unaccusative verbs.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'have come' denotes a punctual boundary transition (arrival at a location/state) without a durative process phase, matching the achievement category exemplified by 'arrive'.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'have come' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (arrival at a location/situation) that can be modified by specific time-point adverbials ('Have you come at this moment?'), does not permit interruption ('one cannot stop coming'), and lacks measurable internal duration, satisfying multiple criteria for achievement aspect.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'have come' denotes a punctual, bounded event with an inherent endpoint (arrival at a location/state), not a dynamic durative activity; it cannot be naturally modified by 'for X time' without semantic oddity and does not exhibit homogeneity across subintervals.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have come' entails a transition into a resulting state (arrival/presence at a location), which violates the semelfactive requirement of no resulting change of state.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'have come' denotes a change-of-state and bounded dynamic event marking the onset of arrival, involving internal eventive unfolding rather than a static condition.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'Have you come to destroy us?' is an open interrogative question about a present/actual situation, not a counterfactual conditional; it expresses uncertainty about the addressee's actual intentions, not a contrary-to-fact condition.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have come' is not negated; the sentence 'Have you come to destroy us?' is a positive interrogative with no negator applying to the target gram.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'Have you come to destroy us?' contains no lexical past time adverb, phrasal past time adverb, or clausal past time anchor; it is a direct question without temporal specification.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'have come' entails a change of location (arrival), and the resulting state of being present/arrived is relevant to the subsequent discourse where the speaker addresses Jesus directly.
DtRes_explanation: The motion verb 'come' in the question 'Have you come to destroy us?' does not entail that the mover remains at a destination; rather, it is a rhetorical question about purpose without indicating a persisting change of location state.
DtUniv_explanation: The verb 'have come' denotes a discrete bounded event (arrival/transition into a new state) rather than a situation holding continuously over time, with no duration adverbial or habitual framing present.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'come' is a transition event (arrival/movement to a location), and 'have come' presents this transition as a completed whole bounded occurrence, matching the set_to_1_if criteria for transition events.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have come' appears in an interrogative clause ('Have you come to destroy us?') that seeks confirmation of an actual fact or realized situation, not a future, hypothetical, or modal projection; interrogative form alone does not trigger irrealis when the question addresses a realized state of affairs.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have come' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by a past participle 'come', with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the set_to_0_if condition.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'have come' is active voice (subject 'you' is the semantic agent performing the action of coming), not passive voice; it lacks the required 'been' + past participle structure for VcPass.

FullText: Jesus rebuked him , saying : » Be quiet and come out of him . « When the demon had thrown him down in front of them , he came out of him without harming him .
ASTrns_explanation: The target verb 'had thrown' introduces both an overt grammatical subject ('the demon') and an overt direct object ('him'), satisfying the first set_to_1_if condition.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'throw' licenses an external argument (the demon as agent) and introduces a direct object (him), satisfying the set_to_0_if conditions.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'thrown' licenses and realizes an internal argument (the direct object 'him'), satisfying the set_to_0_if condition that the target verb licenses or realizes an internal argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'throw down' denotes a punctual or near-instantaneous change-of-state (a boundary transition from upright to fallen position) without a durative process phase, matching the set_to_0_if criterion for achievement verbs.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'had thrown him down' denotes a punctual transition into a new physical state (the subject moving from upright to fallen), naturally modifiable by a time-point adverbial ('at that moment'), and lacks measurable internal duration, satisfying multiple criteria for achievement aspect.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'had thrown him down' denotes a single punctual event with an inherent endpoint (the completion of throwing), not a dynamic durative activity; it cannot be naturally modified by 'for X time' without changing meaning, and the situation lacks homogeneous subintervals.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'had thrown him down' entails a transition into a resulting state (the person being in a thrown-down position), which disqualifies it from semelfactive status.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'had thrown him down' denotes a bounded dynamic event involving change-of-state and internal eventive unfolding (the action of throwing), which is incompatible with stative aspect.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'When the demon had thrown him down in front of them' uses past perfect morphology to express temporal anteriority within a narrative sequence (the throwing occurred before the demon came out), not to mark a counterfactual condition contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'had thrown' is not negated; the negator 'without' in 'without harming him' negates the embedded verb 'harming', not the target gram.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause contains the clausal past time anchor 'When', a temporal conjunction that establishes a specific temporal relationship for the past perfect verb 'had thrown'.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'had thrown him down' denotes a bounded activity that establishes a resulting state (him being on the ground) relevant to the subsequent narrative event ('he came out of him'), making it non-experiential per the edge case rule about narrative progression.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'had thrown him down' describes a motion/action event, but the clause indicates a completed past action within a narrative sequence ('When the demon had thrown him down... he came out of him'), with no entailment that the resulting state (him being down) persists at the reference time; the focus is on the event as a narrative trigger, not on a resulting state currently in effect.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete bounded event (the demon throwing him down) that serves as a temporal marker for another event (he came out), not a situation holding continuously across a time span.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'had thrown him down' is a transition event (throw/cast down) construed as a completed bounded occurrence that serves as a discrete step in the narrative sequence ('When...he came out'), packaging the situation as a bounded move along the discourse timeline.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had thrown' asserts a realized event in the narrative past (the demon threw him down), occurring within a temporal sequence of actual events, not within a modal, conditional, counterfactual, or hypothetical context.
TLTS_explanation: The auxiliary 'had' is morphologically past, making this a past perfect construction that satisfies the first set_to_1_if condition.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had thrown (him) down' is active voice with the grammatical subject 'the demon' as the semantic agent and the object 'him' as the semantic patient; it lacks the passive auxiliary sequence 'have/had' + 'been' + past participle required for VcPass.

FullText: He saw two boats docked by the shore . The fishermen had gone away from them and were washing their nets .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'had gone away' is intransitive and does not introduce an overt direct or indirect object; 'away' is an adverbial modifier, not an internal argument.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'gone away' denotes directed motion/change-of-location where the sole argument (fishermen) undergoes displacement with no external argument licensed, matching the unaccusative criterion for motion verbs.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'had gone away' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('The fishermen') functioning as an external argument engaged in manner of motion (going away) with no internal argument licensed, matching the CRI for manner of motion verbs.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'had gone away' denotes a punctual boundary transition (the moment of departure) rather than a durative process directed toward a defined goal, and cannot be naturally modified by 'in X time' to indicate completion of a bounded accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'had gone away' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (departure from a location), naturally modifiable by time-point adverbials (e.g., 'had gone away at noon'), and lacks measurable internal duration as a boundary event.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'had gone away' denotes a completed change of location with an inherent endpoint (the fishermen are no longer at the boats), satisfying the set_to_0 criterion of entailing a specific, defined result state.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'had gone away' entails a transition into a resulting state (the fishermen are no longer at the boats) and describes a completed action with a change of state, not a single punctual occurrence without resulting change.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'had gone away' denotes a change-of-state and bounded dynamic event (movement from one location to another), which involves internal eventive unfolding and dynamic progression incompatible with stative aspect.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'had gone away' expresses temporal anteriority within a narrative (the fishermen had left before the observation), presenting an actual past event without any counterfactual marking or indication that the situation is contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'had gone away' is not negated; there is no negator ('not', 'n't', 'never') modifying it, no negative quantifier in subject or object position, and no 'neither...nor' construction.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'had gone away' contains no lexical past time adverb, phrasal past time adverb, or clausal past time anchor; 'away' is a directional adverb, not a temporal one.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'had gone away' entails a change of location (from the boats to elsewhere), and that resulting state (fishermen being away from the boats) is relevant at the reference time for the subsequent action of washing nets, making it non-experiential.
DtRes_explanation: The motion verb 'gone away' describes a change of location where the fishermen have moved away from the boats and remain at that destination at the reference time, satisfying the resultative condition for motion verbs.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete bounded event (the fishermen departing from the boats) rather than a situation holding continuously across a time span; the focus is on the transition into a new state of being away.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'gone away' is a transition event (departure/movement away) construed as a completed whole prior to the reference time, presenting a bounded occurrence rather than an ongoing or habitual situation.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had gone away' is a past perfect form asserting a realized state of affairs in the actual world (the fishermen had completed the action of leaving), with no modal, conditional, counterfactual, future, or wish context present.
TLTS_explanation: The auxiliary 'had' is morphologically past, making this a past perfect construction that satisfies the first set_to_1_if condition.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had gone away' lacks the required passive morphology (auxiliary 'have' + 'been' + past participle); 'gone' is the past participle of an intransitive verb, and the subject 'fishermen' is the semantic agent performing the action, not the patient.

FullText: Everyone was amazed and they glorified God . They were filled with fear . They said : » We have seen strange things today . «
ASTrns_explanation: The target verb 'have seen' introduces an overt direct object 'strange things', satisfying the first set_to_1_if condition.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'seen' licenses an external argument (the implicit subject 'we') and introduces a direct object ('strange things'), satisfying the conditions for set_to_0_if.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'have seen' licenses an internal argument ('strange things'), which is a direct object, so it does not meet the criteria for ASUnrg.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'have seen' denotes a punctual perceptual event—the instantaneous transition into visual awareness—without a durative process phase leading to a defined endpoint, matching the achievement profile rather than accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'have seen' denotes a punctual perceptual transition into awareness of 'strange things', satisfying CRI criterion 4 (punctual perceptual/cognitive transition into recognition), and can be modified by the time-point adverbial 'today', satisfying CRI criterion 2.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'have seen' denotes a bounded perceptual event marking the onset of awareness of 'strange things'; even though the things themselves may be extended, the perceptual event of seeing/becoming aware is a single completed occurrence, not a dynamic durative activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The present perfect 'have seen' construes the event as a durative experience extending from the past to the present moment ('today'), not as a single punctual occurrence, and entails a resulting state of having witnessed something.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'have seen' denotes a bounded perceptual event marking the completion of awareness ('saw strange things'), which encodes eventive change rather than a static condition, triggering the set_to_0_if rule for bounded perceptual events.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'We have seen strange things today' presents a situation that actually occurred in the narrative world (the people witnessed strange things), with no indication of counterfactuality, unfulfilled wishes, or contrary-to-fact conditions.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have seen' is not negated; it appears in a positive declarative statement without negation markers, negative quantifiers, or 'never'.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause contains the lexical past time adverb 'today', which establishes a specific temporal anchor for the past event 'have seen'.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'have seen' denotes perception where the focus is on the occurrence of an experience (witnessing strange things) rather than on a resulting state, matching the positive example 'We have seen the Eiffel Tower.'
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'have seen' is a perception verb that does not entail a resulting state persisting at the reference time; it merely reports a past perceptual experience without a change of state or condition currently in effect.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'We have seen strange things today' describes discrete bounded events (witnessing specific strange occurrences) rather than a situation holding continuously across a time span, and the focus is on the completed actions of seeing rather than a non-terminating situation.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'have seen' is a transition event (perceiving/noticing something) construed as a bounded occurrence within the narrative sequence ('today'), presenting the situation as a completed whole rather than ongoing or habitual.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have seen' is a present perfect assertion within direct speech reporting a realized event ('We have seen strange things today'), with no modal, conditional, future, or hypothetical context present.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have seen' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by a past participle with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the set_to_0_if condition.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'have seen' is active voice (subject 'we' is the agent/doer), not passive voice; passive voice requires 'have been' + past participle, not 'have' + past participle alone.

FullText: » Woe to you who are rich ! You have received your reward .

ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'have received' licenses and realizes an internal argument ('your reward' as direct object), satisfying the set_to_0_if condition for transitive verbs.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'receive' denotes a punctual boundary transition (the moment of receiving) without a durative process phase leading to a defined endpoint, matching the achievement profile in set_to_0_if.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'have received' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (acquiring possession of a reward) that can be modified by a specific time-point adverbial and does not permit interruption, satisfying multiple criteria for achievement aspect.

AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have received' entails a transition into a resulting state (possession of reward), which violates the semelfactive criterion of 'no resulting change of state'.

DtExp_explanation: The verb 'had been healed' entails a change of state (from infirm to healed) where the resulting state of wellness persists at the reference time, making it non-experiential.
DtRes_explanation: The construction 'had been healed' is a stative copula (past participle used as adjective in copular form) describing a state rather than encoding a transition; per edge case rules, copular constructions do not encode resultative meaning even when the adjective denotes a resulting condition.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a completed transition into a new state (healing from evil spirits) rather than a situation holding continuously over time, focusing on the resultant condition rather than duration.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'had been healed' is a passive form asserting a bounded transition event (healing as a completed state change), matching the rule for passive bounded transitions.

FullText: Traveling with him were the women who had been healed of evil spirits and infirmities . This included Mary Magdalene , from whom seven demons had gone out .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'had gone out' is intransitive and does not introduce an overt direct or indirect object; 'out' is an adverbial particle indicating direction, not an internal argument.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'gone out' denotes directed motion/change-of-location where the sole argument (seven demons) undergoes displacement with no external argument licensed, matching the unaccusative criterion for change-of-location verbs.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'had gone out' denotes manner of motion with the subject 'seven demons' functioning as the external argument and no internal argument licensed, matching the third CRI criterion for set_to_1.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'had gone out' denotes a punctual boundary transition (demons departing/exiting) without a durative process phase leading to a defined endpoint, matching the achievement profile rather than accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'had gone out' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (demons departing/ceasing to possess), naturally modifiable by time-point adverbials, with no internal duration or interruption phase, meeting multiple criteria for achievement aspect.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'had gone out' denotes a bounded, punctual event with an inherent endpoint (the completion of demons departing), not a dynamic durative activity; it cannot be naturally modified by 'for X time' without implying completion of a goal.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'had gone out' entails a resulting change of state (demons departing from Mary Magdalene), which disqualifies it from semelfactive status according to the set_to_0_if rule.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'had gone out' denotes a bounded change-of-state event (demons departing from a person) involving dynamic progression and eventive unfolding, which sets it to 0 rather than a stative condition.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'from whom seven demons had gone out' uses past perfect morphology to express temporal anteriority within a narrative (the demons had departed before the traveling event), presenting an actual occurrence without any counterfactual marking.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'had gone out' is not negated; there is no negator ('not', 'n't', 'never') or negative quantifier modifying it in the clause 'from whom seven demons had gone out'.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'from whom seven demons had gone out' contains no lexical, phrasal, or clausal past time adverbial; it is a simple past perfect statement without temporal anchoring.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'had gone out' entails a change of state (demons departing from Mary Magdalene) where the resulting state of absence persists at the reference time, making it non-experiential rather than a life-history entry.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'had gone out' describes a change of state (demons departing from Mary Magdalene) where the resulting state—that the demons are no longer in her—persists at the reference time, satisfying the motion verb criterion that the mover has moved to a destination and remains there.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'seven demons had gone out' describes a discrete bounded event (the departure of demons) rather than a situation holding continuously over time, and the verb 'gone out' entails a transition into a new state.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'had gone out' is a transition event (departure/exit) construed as a completed whole, presenting a bounded occurrence rather than an ongoing or stative situation.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had gone out' asserts a realized past event (demons departed from Mary Magdalene) as an actual fact within the narrative world, with no modal, conditional, future, or hypothetical context present.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'had gone out' is morphologically past (auxiliary 'had' + past participle 'gone'), satisfying the first condition in set_to_1_if.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had gone out' lacks the required passive morphology (auxiliary 'have' + 'been' + past participle); 'gone' is the past participle of the intransitive verb 'go', not a transitive verb in passive voice, and the subject 'seven demons' is the semantic agent/theme undergoing the action, not a patient affected by an external agent.

FullText: » Those by the side of the road are they who have heard . Then the devil comes and takes the word from their hearts that they may not believe and be saved .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'have heard' does not introduce an overt internal argument (direct or indirect object); the object is implied but not syntactically realized, matching the pattern 'He has eaten' from the negative examples.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'heard' licenses an external argument (the implicit subject 'they' who perform the act of hearing), making it an agentive transitive verb that does not meet unaccusative criteria.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'have heard' licenses an internal argument (the word, understood from context as the direct object of 'heard'), thus satisfying the set_to_0_if condition that the target verb licenses or realizes an internal argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'have heard' denotes a punctual perceptual event—a near-instantaneous transition into awareness—without a durative process phase leading to a defined endpoint, matching the achievement profile rather than accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'have heard' denotes a punctual perceptual transition into awareness (hearing the word), which is instantaneous and does not permit interruption, matching the achievement criterion for cognitive/perceptual verbs that mark a single transition into recognition.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'have heard' denotes a bounded perceptual event marking the onset of awareness (hearing the word), which is treated as a single completed occurrence rather than a dynamic durative activity, matching the set_to_0_if criterion for bounded perceptual events.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have heard' entails a transition into a resulting state (the state of having received and retained information), which disqualifies it from semelfactive status according to the set_to_0_if criteria.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'have heard' denotes a bounded perceptual event marking the onset of awareness (hearing/receiving the word), which is eventive change rather than a static condition, matching the set_to_0_if criterion for bounded perceptual events.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'those who have heard' presents the situation as having actually occurred in the actual world (people have heard the word), without any indication that it is contrary to fact, matching the set_to_0_if criterion for factual past situations.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have heard' is not negated; the negation 'not' in the sentence applies to 'believe' in the embedded clause 'that they may not believe', not to the target gram.
DtDTAM_explanation: The target clause 'have heard' contains no lexical, phrasal, or clausal past time adverbial; the 'Then' that follows is a logical connector ('therefore') introducing a consequence, not a temporal anchor for the target clause.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'have heard' denotes perception where the focus is on the occurrence of the experience (hearing the word) at least once in the subject's history, rather than on a resulting state, matching the CRI for perception verbs and the positive example 'We have seen the Eiffel Tower.'
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'have heard' describes a perception event (hearing) without entailing a resulting state that persists at the reference time; the clause merely reports a past interaction without a persisting condition, similar to the negative example 'He has given me advice before.'
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'have heard' describes a discrete, bounded event of receiving/perceiving the word, not a situation extending over time; the focus is on the completed action of hearing rather than a continuous state or habitual series.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'have heard' denotes a stative situation (the state of having received auditory input) without a construed boundary or transition, and the clause does not present a bounded occurrence but rather a persistent state.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have heard' asserts a realized state of affairs in the actual world (those people have actually heard the word), with no modal auxiliary, conditional, counterfactual, or future marking present.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have heard' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by a past participle with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the set_to_0_if condition.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'have heard' is active voice (auxiliary 'have' + past participle of transitive verb 'hear'), where the grammatical subject 'they' is the semantic agent (doer) who performed the hearing, not the patient; passive voice requires 'have been' + past participle, not 'have' + past participle alone.

FullText: When he arrived on the shore , a demon-possessed man met him . He wore no clothes and did not live in a house but in the tombs . He had been this way a long time .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'had been' functions as a copula linking the subject 'He' to the predicative complement 'this way', introducing no internal argument (direct or indirect object).
ASUncc_explanation: The copular clause 'had been this way' uses 'be' as a copula with no external argument licensed; the sole argument functions as the syntactic subject linked to a predicative complement ('this way'), matching the copular unaccusative pattern.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'had been' denotes existence or a state of being (copular), which does not project an external argument but rather describes the subject's state, falling under the set_to_0_if condition for existence/appearance verbs.
AktAcc_explanation: The phrase 'had been this way' denotes a state or condition without an inherent endpoint or durative process directed toward a defined goal; it describes a persistent state that naturally takes 'for X time' modification ('for a long time') rather than 'in X time', marking it as a stative rather than accomplishment aspect.
AktAch_explanation: The event 'had been this way' denotes a state or condition persisting over measurable duration ('a long time'), not a punctual transition into a new state, and permits interruption/continuation of an ongoing process phase.
AktAct_explanation: The phrase 'had been this way' denotes a dynamic, durative situation of ongoing existence in a particular state/condition that can be naturally modified by 'a long time' (durative adverbial), satisfying the criteria for activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'had been this way' entails a durative state persisting over measurable time ('a long time'), not a single punctual occurrence, thus does not instantiate the semelfactive feature.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'had been this way' is morphologically marked as progressive perfect aspect ('had been'), which entails dynamic unfolding and is explicitly incompatible with stative readings per the set_to_0_if rules.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'He had been this way a long time' uses past perfect to express temporal anteriority within a narrative (the demon-possessed man's state prior to the arrival), presenting the situation as factually occurred without any counterfactual marking.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'had been' is not negated; the negation in the sentence ('did not live') applies to a different verb and does not negate the eventuality of 'had been this way'.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause contains the phrasal past time adverb 'a long time', which establishes a specific temporal duration in the past.
DtExp_explanation: The clause 'He had been this way a long time' contains a duration adverbial ('a long time') indicating a continuing situation rather than a completed life experience, which triggers the set_to_0 rule.
DtRes_explanation: The stative copula 'had been this way' describes a persisting condition rather than a transition to a resulting state, and the duration adverbial 'a long time' indicates a continuing situation rather than a completed change of state.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'had been this way a long time' contains a duration adverbial ('a long time') and denotes a non-terminating state that held continuously throughout the entire time span, satisfying the criteria for DtUniv.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'He had been this way a long time' denotes a stative situation (a persistent condition or state) without a construed boundary, and the duration adverbial 'a long time' profiles the internal temporal span rather than packaging the situation as a bounded whole.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had been this way' asserts a realized state of affairs in the past (the man's condition had persisted for a long time), with no modal, conditional, future, or hypothetical context present.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'had been' includes both a morphologically past auxiliary ('had') and 'been' followed by a predicative adjective/adverbial phrase, matching the past perfect progressive structure that triggers TLTS marking.
VcPass_explanation: The complement of 'been' is the adverbial phrase 'this way' (not a past participle), making 'been' a lexical copula rather than a passive auxiliary; therefore VcPass is not instantiated.

FullText: Jesus asked him : » What is your name ? « He answered : » Legion « for many demons had entered into him .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'had entered into' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('many demons') and an overt internal argument ('him'), satisfying the condition for marking 1.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'enter' licenses an external argument (the demons as agent/initiator that actively entered into him), making it unergative rather than unaccusative.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'entered into' licenses an internal argument ('him'), making it transitive and thus not an unergative structure.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'entered into' denotes a punctual boundary transition (crossing a threshold into a location/state) without a durative process phase, matching the achievement profile rather than accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'had entered into' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (demons moving from outside into the possessed person), naturally modifiable by a time-point adverbial, with no measurable internal duration and no interruption-permitting process phase.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'had entered into' denotes a punctual event with an inherent endpoint (the completion of entering), not a dynamic durative activity; it cannot be naturally modified by 'for X time' without implying completion, and the situation is bounded rather than homogeneous.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'had entered into him' entails a transition into a new resulting state (demons being inside him), which disqualifies it from semelfactive status according to the set_to_0_if criteria.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'had entered into' denotes a change-of-state event with a clear boundary marking the onset of demons entering the person, which is a bounded dynamic event incompatible with stative aspect.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'many demons had entered into him' presents a situation understood as having actually occurred in the narrative world (the demons did enter him), using past perfect for temporal anteriority within the story, not expressing a counterfactual condition.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'had entered into' is not negated; the sentence is affirmative and describes demons actually entering into him.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'many demons had entered into him' contains no lexical past time adverb, phrasal past time adverb, or clausal past time anchor; 'for' is a causal connector, not a temporal one.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'had entered into' entails a change of location/state (demons entering the subject), and that resulting state (demons being inside him) persists at the reference time, making this non-experiential.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'had entered into' describes a change of possession/state (demons entering him) where the resulting state—that demons are currently inside him—persists at the reference time, as evidenced by the present-tense context explaining his condition.
DtUniv_explanation: The verb 'had entered into' denotes a transition into a new state (demons entering the person), focusing on the bounded event of entry rather than a situation holding continuously across a time span.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'had entered into' is a transition event (enter = change of location/state) construed as a completed whole, presenting a single bounded occurrence with a construed endpoint rather than an ongoing or habitual situation.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had entered into (him)' is a past perfect form asserting a realized state of affairs in the actual world within the narrative context; it is not embedded in a modal, conditional, future, or wish construction.
TLTS_explanation: The auxiliary 'had' is morphologically past, making this a past perfect construction that satisfies the first set_to_1_if condition.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had entered into' lacks the required passive morphology (auxiliary 'have' + 'been' + past participle); it is an active voice construction where the grammatical subject 'demons' is the semantic agent performing the action of entering.

FullText: They went out to see what happened . They came to Jesus and found the man who had been demon possessed . He was clothed , in his right mind and sitting at the feet of Jesus . They were afraid .
ASTrns_explanation: The clause is morphologically passive ('had been possessed') and the lexical verb 'possess' licenses both an external argument (agent/demon, implied) and an internal argument (patient: 'the man'), satisfying the passive construction rule.
ASUncc_explanation: The clause is morphologically passive ('had been possessed'), and the lexical verb 'possess' licenses an external argument (the demon/demonic force as the implicit agent), thus satisfying the set_to_0_if condition for passive clauses with licensed external arguments.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'possessed' is a past participle functioning as an adjective in a passive construction; the man is the internal argument (theme/patient) undergoing the state of being possessed, not an external argument performing an action.
AktAcc_explanation: The state 'demon possessed' is a resultant condition without an inherent durative process of becoming possessed; the possession itself is punctual/instantaneous, and the perfect aspect 'had been possessed' describes entry into a state rather than a bounded accomplishment with measurable duration toward a goal.
AktAch_explanation: The phrase 'had been demon possessed' denotes a state or condition that unfolded over measurable duration rather than a punctual transition, and does not entail a single boundary event but rather describes an ongoing state prior to the narrative moment.
AktAct_explanation: The situation denotes a bounded result state (being possessed) with a defined endpoint, lacks dynamic unfolding, and is treated as a single completed occurrence rather than an ongoing activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The event entails a transition into a resulting state (possession) and is construed as a durative condition rather than a single punctual occurrence, thus not meeting the semelfactive criteria.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'had been demon possessed' is marked with progressive aspect ('had been'), which entails dynamic eventive unfolding and is incompatible with stative readings per CRI set_to_0_if rule 2.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'who had been demon possessed' uses past perfect morphology to express temporal anteriority within a narrative (the possession occurred before they found him), describing an actual event that happened in the story world, with no indication of counterfactuality.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'had been demon possessed' contains no negation marker ('not', 'n't'), negative quantifier, 'never', or 'neither...nor' construction; the sentence is affirmative.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'who had been demon possessed' contains no lexical past time adverb, phrasal past time adverb, or clausal past time anchor; it is a relative clause describing a state without temporal specification.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'had been demon possessed' entails a change of state (from possessed to not possessed), and the resulting state of being no longer possessed is relevant to the narrative context where the man is now 'clothed, in his right mind' sitting at Jesus's feet, making this a state-change rather than an experiential entry.
DtRes_explanation: The construction 'had been demon possessed' is a stative copula describing a prior condition rather than encoding a transition to a resulting state that persists at reference time; the subsequent clauses ('He was clothed, in his right mind') indicate the man is no longer in that possessed state.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete bounded state (demon possession) that has ended, with focus on the transition from possessed to no longer possessed (now clothed and in his right mind), rather than a situation holding continuously across a time span.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'had been demon possessed' uses the passive form 'have been' + past participle and asserts a bounded transition event (the state of possession as a completed condition that has now changed, evidenced by the following clause 'He was clothed, in his right mind'), packaging it as a discrete bounded occurrence rather than an ongoing process.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had been demon possessed' asserts a realized state of affairs in the actual world—the man's past condition of possession is presented as a fact that occurred and was discovered, not as hypothetical, conditional, or uncertain.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'had been (demon) possessed' contains the morphologically past auxiliary 'had' followed by 'been' and a participle, matching the criteria for past perfect progressive marking.
VcPass_explanation: The complement of 'been' is the adjectival phrase 'demon possessed' (a non-verbal predicate describing a state), not a verbal past participle functioning as passive; 'been' functions as a copula here, not a passive auxiliary.

FullText: » Return to your house and declare the great things God has done for you . « He went throughout the whole city telling the great things Jesus had done for him .

ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'done' licenses an internal argument ('the great things'), making it transitive and thus not an unergative verb.

ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has done' is not negated; there is no negator ('not', 'n't', 'never') modifying it, no negative quantifier in subject or object position, and no 'neither...nor' construction present in the clause.
DtDTAM_explanation: The target clause 'God has done for you' contains no temporal adverbial (lexical, phrasal, or clausal) that establishes a specific past time anchor.

DtRes_explanation: The verb 'has done' reports past actions/miracles without entailing a persisting resulting state; it is a communication context where the speaker is asked to 'declare' these things, indicating they are being reported as past events rather than states currently in effect.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes discrete bounded events (the great things God has done) rather than a situation holding continuously across a time span, and the verb 'has done' entails completed actions without focus on duration.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'has done' presents a transition event (the accomplishment of great things) as a single bounded occurrence with a construed endpoint, rather than as ongoing or habitual.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has done' asserts a realized situation in the actual world (God's past actions are presented as factual events), with no modal, conditional, future, or wish context present.

VcPass_explanation: The target 'has done' is active voice (auxiliary 'have' + past participle 'done' of transitive verb 'do'), with 'God' as the semantic agent and 'things' as the semantic patient in object position, not passive voice requiring 'have been' + past participle.

FullText: » Return to your house and declare the great things God has done for you . « He went throughout the whole city telling the great things Jesus had done for him .

FullText: He said to her : » Daughter , your faith has made you whole ; go in peace . «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has made' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('your faith') and an overt direct object ('you'), satisfying the first set_to_1_if condition.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'made' licenses an external argument (your faith as agent/initiator) and introduces a direct object (you), satisfying the set_to_0_if conditions.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'has made' licenses an internal argument (direct object 'you'), satisfying the set_to_0_if condition that the target verb licenses or realizes an internal argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'made whole' denotes a punctual change-of-state or boundary transition (from not-whole to whole) without a durative process phase, similar to 'decide' or 'arrive', and does not naturally accept 'in X time' modification in this context.
