AktState_explanation: The verb 'would have repented' denotes a bounded change-of-state event (transition from non-repentance to repentance) involving dynamic eventive unfolding, which is incompatible with AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The gram 'would have repented' occurs in the apodosis (result clause) of a counterfactual conditional introduced by 'If the mighty works had been done in Tyre and Sidon', expressing an unfulfilled past condition contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'would have repented' is not negated; it appears in a conditional clause expressing a counterfactual positive outcome, and no negator (not, n't, never, or negative quantifier) directly negates this verb.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause contains the lexical past time adverb 'long ago', which is a specific temporal anchor indicating a past time reference.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'would have repented' denotes a change of state (from not repenting to repenting) where the resulting state of repentance would hold at the reference time, which matches the set_to_0_if criterion for verbs entailing a change of state.
DtRes_explanation: The clause is counterfactual (conditional with 'if' and 'would have'), negating the actual occurrence of the event; no transition to a resulting state is entailed in the real world at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete bounded event (repentance as a transition into a new state) in a counterfactual conditional, not a situation holding continuously across a time span.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The target 'would have repented' presents a transition event (repent = change of state) as a bounded, completed occurrence in a counterfactual conditional, construed as a single whole event rather than ongoing or habitual.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'would have repented' is a modal auxiliary construction (would + have + past participle) appearing in the consequent of a counterfactual conditional ('If the mighty works had been done...they would have repented'), presenting a hypothetical situation contrary to fact rather than a realized state.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'would have repented' includes a modal auxiliary ('would') preceding 'have', which matches the third set_to_1_if condition.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'would have repented' is active voice (conditional perfect of a transitive verb with the grammatical subject 'they' as the semantic agent), not passive voice, which requires the auxiliary sequence 'have' + 'been' + past participle.

FullText: » Behold , I have given you authority to tread upon serpents and scorpions , and over all the power of the enemy : and nothing shall in any way hurt you .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'have given' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('I') and an overt direct object ('you'), satisfying the first set_to_1_if condition for ASTrns.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'have given' licenses an external argument (the implicit agent 'I') and introduces a direct object ('you authority'), satisfying the set_to_0_if conditions.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'have given' licenses and realizes internal arguments ('you' as indirect object and 'authority' as direct object), satisfying the set_to_0_if condition for transitive verbs with objects.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'give' denotes a punctual transfer of authority without a durative process phase; it is a boundary transition event that occurs instantaneously rather than unfolding toward a defined endpoint.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'have given' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (conferral of authority), lacks measurable internal duration, and naturally occurs at a defined temporal boundary, satisfying the achievement criteria.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'have given' denotes a completed transfer of authority with a defined result state (you now possess authority), not a dynamic durative activity; it entails a specific endpoint and cannot be naturally modified by 'for X time' without implying incompleteness.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have given (you) authority' entails a transition into a resulting state where the recipient now possesses authority, which was not the case before, thus failing the semelfactive criterion of 'no resulting change of state'.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'have given' denotes a completed transfer of authority (change-of-state event with a clear boundary), not a static condition or mental state that holds over time.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'I have given you authority' presents the situation as actually occurring in the actual world (a statement of fact by the speaker), without any indication that it is contrary to fact, counterfactual, or expressing an unfulfilled wish.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have given' is not negated; the negation 'nothing shall in any way hurt you' applies to a different eventuality (hurt), not to the giving of authority.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause contains no lexical past time adverb, phrasal past time adverb, or clausal past time anchor; the temporal adverbial requirement for DtDTAM is not met.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'have given' entails a change of possession and social/legal status (granting authority) that remains in effect at the reference time, matching the set_to_0_if criterion.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'have given' describes a change of possession/status (granting authority), and the clause entails that this authority is currently held and in effect, as evidenced by the immediately following infinitive 'to tread upon serpents and scorpions,' which indicates the authority is presently operative.
DtUniv_explanation: The verb 'have given' denotes a completed transfer of authority (a transition into a new state), not a situation holding continuously over time, and lacks duration adverbials indicating extension to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'give authority' is a transition event (transfer of possession/rights) construed as a completed whole, presenting a single bounded occurrence with a construed endpoint rather than an ongoing or habitual situation.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have given (you) authority' is a present perfect that asserts a realized action in the actual world; it is not embedded in a modal, conditional, future, or optative context, and thus does not instantiate irrealis.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have given' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by a past participle 'given', with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the set_to_0_if condition.
VcPass_explanation: The construction 'have given' is active voice with the grammatical subject 'I' as the semantic agent and 'you' as the semantic patient (object); it lacks the passive auxiliary sequence 'have been' + past participle required for VcPass.

FullText: » All things have been delivered to me from my Father . No one knows who the Son is except the Father . And no one knows who the Father is except the Son and those to whom the Son is willing to reveal him . «
ASTrns_explanation: The clause is morphologically passive ('have been delivered'), and the lexical verb 'deliver' licenses both an external argument (agent: 'my Father') and an internal argument (patient: 'All things'), satisfying the passive construction rule.
ASUncc_explanation: The clause is morphologically passive ('have been delivered'), and the lexical verb 'deliver' licenses an external argument (the Father as agent, whether suppressed or implied), triggering the set_to_0_if condition for passive clauses.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'have been delivered' is passive and licenses an internal argument ('All things'), which functions as the theme/patient of the delivery event, satisfying the set_to_0_if condition for verbs that license or realize internal arguments.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'delivered' denotes a punctual transfer or boundary transition (movement of things from Father to Son) without a durative process phase, matching the achievement profile rather than accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'delivered' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (transfer of possession/authority from Father to Son), naturally modifiable by time-point adverbials, and does not permit interruption of an ongoing process phase.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'have been delivered' denotes a completed transfer with a defined result state (things now possessed by the subject), satisfying the set_to_0 criterion of entailing a specific, inherent endpoint.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have been delivered' entails a resulting change of state (things transitioning from not-delivered to delivered), which disqualifies it from semelfactive status.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'have been delivered' is morphologically marked as progressive perfect aspect ('have been' + past participle), which entails dynamic eventive unfolding and change-of-state (things moving from not-delivered to delivered state), incompatible with stative readings.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'All things have been delivered to me from my Father' presents a situation as actually occurring in the actual world without any counterfactual marking, inverted conditional structure, or indication of unfulfilled wishes.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have been delivered' is not negated; the sentence is positive affirmative ('All things have been delivered to me from my Father').
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause contains no lexical past time adverb, phrasal past time adverb, or clausal past time anchor; the passive construction 'have been delivered' lacks any temporal specification.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'have been delivered' entails a change of possession/transfer where the resulting state (things now possessed by the Son) holds at the reference time, matching the set_to_0_if criterion.
DtRes_explanation: The passive construction 'have been delivered' describes a change of possession (things transferred from Father to Son) where the resulting state of possession is currently held at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a completed transfer of discrete entities (things delivered from Father to Son), focusing on the resultant state of transfer rather than a situation holding continuously over time, matching the pattern of punctual/transitional events in the negative examples.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'have been delivered' is a passive form asserting a bounded transition event (delivery as a completed whole) rather than an ongoing process, matching the rule for passive bounded transitions.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have been delivered' is a present perfect passive asserting a realized state of affairs in the actual world with no modal, conditional, future, or hypothetical context present.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have been delivered' includes 'been' followed by a past participle (progressive-like structure with passive voice), which matches the criterion for setting to 1.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'have been delivered' instantiates the form auxiliary 'have' + 'been' + past participle of transitive verb 'deliver', the grammatical subject 'All things' corresponds to the semantic patient (entities affected), and the 'by'-phrase 'from my Father' expresses the external agent.

FullText: » However one thing is needed . Mary has chosen the proper way and it shall not be taken away from her . «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has chosen' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('Mary') and an overt direct object ('the proper way'), satisfying the first set_to_1_if condition.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'has chosen' licenses an external argument (Mary as agent) and introduces a direct object ('the proper way'), satisfying the set_to_0_if conditions.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'has chosen' licenses and realizes an internal argument ('the proper way' as direct object), satisfying the set_to_0_if condition for transitive verbs.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'choose' denotes a punctual mental act of decision that occurs at a single moment without a durative process phase leading to a defined endpoint, matching the achievement category.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'has chosen' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (the state of having made a decision), can be modified by a specific time-point adverbial (e.g., 'Mary has chosen at that moment'), does not permit interruption of an ongoing process phase, and lacks measurable internal duration, satisfying multiple criteria for achievement aspect.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'has chosen' denotes a punctual, bounded event with an inherent endpoint (the completion of the choice), lacks dynamic durative unfolding, and cannot naturally be modified by 'for X time' without implying incompleteness, thus failing all criteria for AktAct.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has chosen' entails a transition into a resulting state (Mary moving from not having chosen to having chosen), which disqualifies it from semelfactive status according to the set_to_0_if criteria.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'has chosen' denotes a bounded dynamic event involving a change-of-state (the transition from not having chosen to having chosen), which represents eventive unfolding rather than a static condition.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'Mary has chosen the proper way' presents the situation as having actually occurred in the actual world without any indication of counterfactuality, contrary to fact status, or unfulfilled wishes.
ClNegPol_explanation: The negation 'shall not be taken away' applies to the passive construction 'be taken away', not to the target gram 'has chosen', which remains positive.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'Mary has chosen the proper way' contains no lexical, phrasal, or clausal past time adverbial; 'However' is a logical connector, not a temporal anchor.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'has chosen' entails a change of state (Mary's selection of a way/path) that results in a persisting state relevant to the discourse ('it shall not be taken away from her'), making this a resultative perfect rather than an experiential one.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'has chosen' describes a change of decision/commitment (Mary's selection of the proper way), and the resulting state (her choice) persists at the reference time, as evidenced by the consequent clause 'it shall not be taken away from her' which affirms the current validity of her choice.
DtUniv_explanation: The verb 'has chosen' denotes a discrete, bounded event (a single act of choosing) resulting in a new state, not a situation extending continuously over time, and lacks duration adverbials or habitual markers.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'chosen' is a transition event (selecting/deciding) construed as a completed whole, presenting Mary's action as a single bounded occurrence rather than ongoing or habitual.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has chosen' asserts Mary's action as a realized fact in the actual world, with no modal, conditional, future, or wish context present.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has chosen' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by a past participle with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the set_to_0_if condition.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has chosen' is active voice (subject Mary is the agent/doer), not passive voice; it lacks the required 'have + been + past participle' structure for VcPass.

FullText: » If I use the finger power Spirit of God to cast out demons , then the kingdom of God has come upon you ?
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has come' introduces an overt internal argument 'upon you' (prepositional phrase functioning as a locative complement that specifies the affected entity/location), satisfying the requirement for an overt grammatical object or internal argument.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'come' denotes directed motion or change-of-location (arrival of the kingdom) where the sole argument (kingdom of God) undergoes displacement/appearance with no external argument licensed, matching the unaccusative criterion for change-of-location verbs.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'has come' denotes a change-of-location/appearance event where the subject (kingdom of God) functions as an internal argument (theme) undergoing a change of state rather than as an external argument initiating an activity, matching the set_to_0_if criterion for change-of-state/appearance verbs.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'come' denotes a punctual boundary transition (arrival at a location or state) without a durative process phase, matching the achievement category exemplified by 'arrive'.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'has come upon' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (arrival/occurrence of the kingdom), can be modified by specific time-point adverbials ('has come upon you at that moment'), lacks measurable internal duration, and is construed as occurring at a temporal boundary.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'has come upon you' denotes a punctual event with a specific, inherent endpoint (the arrival/onset of the kingdom), marking a single completed occurrence rather than a dynamic durative situation.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has come upon you' entails a transition into a new resulting state (the kingdom of God arriving/being present), which violates the semelfactive requirement of no resulting change of state.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'has come' denotes a bounded change-of-state event marking the arrival/onset of the kingdom of God, involving dynamic progression and an event boundary, which is incompatible with stative aspect.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'the kingdom of God has come upon you' presents a situation as actually occurring (open conditional protasis with present perfect indicating real consequence), not as contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The sentence contains no negator (not, n't, never, or negative quantifier) that negates the target gram 'has come'; the question is posed affirmatively without negation of the auxiliary or the eventuality.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause contains a clausal past time anchor introduced by 'if' paired with 'then', where 'then' functions as a sequential temporal connector meaning 'at that time' (establishing a temporal relationship between the condition and the result), satisfying the rule for clausal temporal anchors.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'has come upon you' entails a change of state (the kingdom of God arriving/being present), and that resulting state holds at the reference time, matching the set_to_0_if criterion.
DtRes_explanation: The motion verb 'come' describes arrival at a destination (upon you), and the clause entails that the kingdom of God has moved to and remains at that destination at the reference time, satisfying the resultative condition for motion verbs.
DtUniv_explanation: The verb 'has come' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (the arrival/establishment of the kingdom of God) rather than a situation holding continuously over time, and there is no duration adverbial indicating extension to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'come' is a transition event (arrival/appearance) construed as a completed whole, presenting the kingdom of God as having arrived at a bounded moment rather than as an ongoing or habitual situation.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has come' appears in the consequent of a conditional construction ('If...then the kingdom of God has come upon you'), presenting the situation as a projected or hypothetical outcome dependent on the condition, rather than as a realized fact in the actual world.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has come' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by a past participle 'come', with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the set_to_0_if condition.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has come' lacks the required passive morphology (auxiliary 'have' + 'been' + past participle); 'come' is an intransitive verb in active voice with 'kingdom of God' as the semantic agent/subject, not a patient.

FullText: » Woe to you Pharisees ! You tithe mint and rue and every herb , and pass over justice and the love of God . You should have done these but you leave them undone .
ASTrns_explanation: The target verb 'should have done' introduces an overt direct object 'these', satisfying the first set_to_1_if condition.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'do' licenses an external argument (the implicit agent 'you') and introduces a direct object ('these'), satisfying the conditions for set_to_0_if.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'done' licenses an internal argument ('these'), which is a direct object, thus failing the requirement for ASUnrg that the verb must not license or realize an internal argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The event 'do these [things]' involves directed action toward a defined goal (practicing justice and love of God) with an inherent endpoint or completion state, satisfying the accomplishment criterion of a durative process directed toward a defined result.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'do' in 'should have done these' denotes a punctual transition into a completed state of action, naturally modifiable by time-point adverbials (e.g., 'should have done these at that moment'), and does not permit interruption as an ongoing process.
AktAct_explanation: The modal construction 'should have done these' denotes a bounded, completed action with a defined result state (the doing of specific things), not a dynamic durative activity; it lacks the homogeneity and natural 'for X time' modification characteristic of activities.
AktSemel_explanation: The target 'should have done these' refers to performing justice and love of God, which entails a transition into a resulting state of having fulfilled moral obligations, not a single punctual occurrence without change of state.
AktState_explanation: The modal construction 'should have done' denotes a bounded dynamic event (the completion of an action) involving internal eventive unfolding and change-of-state, which is incompatible with stative aspect.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The gram 'should have done' occurs in a clause expressing an unfulfilled obligation or regret ('You should have done these but you leave them undone'), where the contrastive marker 'but' explicitly indicates the action did not occur, satisfying the counterfactual condition.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'should have done' is not negated; the negation in the sentence ('leave them undone') applies to a different eventuality, not to the modal construction itself.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'You should have done these' contains no lexical past time adverb, phrasal past time adverb, or clausal past time anchor; it is a counterfactual conditional statement without temporal specification.
DtExp_explanation: The modal 'should have done' expresses a counterfactual obligation or prescriptive claim about what ought to have been done, not an experiential claim about whether an event occurred in the subject's history; it lacks the experiential focus required by DtExp.
DtRes_explanation: The modal construction 'should have done' expresses a counterfactual obligation or recommendation about a past action that was not performed; it does not entail a resulting state currently in effect, as the clause explicitly states 'you leave them undone,' negating any persisting consequence of the action.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'You should have done these' describes a discrete bounded action (doing justice and love of God) that should have occurred, not a situation holding continuously over time; it expresses a counterfactual obligation regarding specific actions rather than duration or habituality.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The modal 'should have done' expresses a counterfactual obligation or unrealized expectation rather than asserting a bounded completed event; it does not present the situation as a single bounded occurrence that actually occurred, but rather as something that ought to have happened but did not.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'should have done' contains the modal auxiliary 'should' combined with the perfect infinitive 'have done', presenting a hypothetical obligation or counterfactual evaluation of what ought to have occurred but did not, rather than asserting a realized situation.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'should have done' includes a modal auxiliary ('should') preceding 'have', which matches the third set_to_1_if condition.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'should have done' lacks the required passive morphology (auxiliary 'have' + 'been' + past participle); it is active voice with 'done' as a transitive verb taking 'these' as its grammatical object, not a passive construction.

FullText: » That which you have said in the darkness will be heard in the light . That which you have spoken in the inner chambers will be proclaimed on the housetops .
ASTrns_explanation: The target verb 'have said' introduces an overt internal argument ('that which you have said'), satisfying the requirement for an overt direct object.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'said' licenses an external argument (the implicit agent 'you' who performs the action of saying), and the clause contains an overt direct object ('that which' / the content said), so it does not meet the criteria for unaccusativity.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'have said' licenses an internal argument (the content clause 'that which you have said in the darkness'), making it transitive and thus not an unergative verb.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'have said' denotes a punctual speech act that is instantaneous or near-instantaneous in its completion, lacking a durative process phase directed toward a defined endpoint, and cannot naturally be modified by 'in X time' to indicate bounded completion.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'have said' denotes a punctual transition into a new state of utterance completion and can be modified by specific time-point adverbials (e.g., 'said at midnight'), meeting the criteria for achievement aspect.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'have said' denotes a completed speech act with a specific, bounded result state (words uttered), lacks dynamic durative unfolding, and cannot naturally take 'for X time' modification without implying incompleteness, matching the set_to_0 criteria for bounded perceptual/communicative events.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have said' entails a transition into a resulting state (words having been uttered and existing as a completed utterance), which disqualifies it from semelfactive status.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'have said' denotes a bounded perceptual/communicative event marking the completion of speech, which is a dynamic change-of-state event rather than a static condition holding over time.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause presents situations as having actually occurred in the actual world (things that were said in darkness and spoken in inner chambers), without any indication of counterfactuality, contrary-to-fact conditions, or unfulfilled wishes.
ClNegPol_explanation: No negator (not, n't, never, or negative quantifier) directly negates the target gram 'have said'; the sentence is entirely positive in polarity.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause contains no lexical past time adverb, phrasal past time adverb, or clausal past time anchor; the temporal references ('in the darkness', 'in the light', 'in the inner chambers', 'on the housetops') are locative/spatial, not temporal.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'have said' denotes communication where the focus is on the occurrence of the speech act as an experience in the subject's history, rather than on a resulting state or persistent consequence.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'have said' is a communication verb that merely reports a past utterance without entailing that the information or directive is currently in effect; the clause describes what was said in darkness, not a persisting state of the utterance itself.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes discrete bounded events (saying something in darkness) with focus on the action itself rather than a situation holding continuously over time, and lacks duration adverbials indicating extension to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'have said' is a transition event (speech act with a construed endpoint) presented as a bounded occurrence that will have consequences ('will be heard'), functioning as a discrete step in a narrative/logical sequence.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have said' asserts a realized situation in the actual world (what you have said in darkness is a completed fact), with no modal auxiliary, conditional, counterfactual, or future marking present.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have said' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by a past participle with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the set_to_0_if condition.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'have said' is active voice (auxiliary 'have' + past participle of transitive verb 'said'), where the grammatical subject 'you' is the semantic agent (speaker), not the patient; passive voice requires 'have been' + past participle.

FullText: » That which you have said in the darkness will be heard in the light . That which you have spoken in the inner chambers will be proclaimed on the housetops .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'have spoken' lacks an overt direct or indirect object; the prepositional phrase 'in the inner chambers' is locative, not an internal argument, making this an intransitive activity reading.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'have spoken' licenses an external argument (the implicit agent 'you' who performs the speaking action), making it unergative rather than unaccusative.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'have spoken' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('you') functioning as an external argument (agent/initiator) engaged in the activity of speaking, with no internal argument (direct or indirect object) licensed or realized.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'have spoken' denotes a punctual or near-instantaneous act of utterance without a durative process phase leading to a defined endpoint; it cannot naturally be modified by 'in X time' to indicate completion of a bounded event, and lacks the gradual change-of-state or directed motion characteristics required for AktAcc.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'have spoken' denotes a durative activity of speech that unfolds over measurable duration rather than a punctual transition into a new state, and it does not entail a result state comparable to achievements like 'arrive' or 'find'.

AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have spoken' entails a transition into a resulting state (words having been uttered and existing as a completed action with consequences), which disqualifies it from semelfactive status.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'have spoken' denotes a bounded perceptual/communicative event marking the completion of speech, which is a dynamic activity involving internal development and eventive unfolding, not a static condition.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause presents the situation as having actually occurred in the actual world (past actions that were said/spoken), without any indication of counterfactuality, contrary-to-fact condition, or unfulfilled wish.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have spoken' is not negated; no negator ('not', 'n't', 'never') directly modifies it, and no negative quantifier negates the clause containing it.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'That which you have spoken in the inner chambers' contains no lexical past time adverb, phrasal past time adverb, or clausal past time anchor; 'in the inner chambers' is a locative adverbial, not a temporal one.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'have spoken' denotes communication where the focus is on the resulting state (words spoken in inner chambers that will be proclaimed), not on the occurrence as a life experience, and the clause emphasizes the persistence and future consequences of what was spoken rather than entry into experiential history.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'have spoken' reports a past communication event without entailing that the spoken words are currently in effect or binding; it merely describes a past interaction that will have future consequences (being proclaimed), not a persisting resulting state at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes discrete bounded events (things spoken in inner chambers) that will be proclaimed, focusing on the transition from private speech to public proclamation, rather than a situation holding continuously over time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'have spoken' denotes a stative situation (speech act resulting in a state of having been said) without a construed boundary or transition endpoint, and the clause does not present the situation as a single bounded occurrence but rather as an unbounded state.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have spoken' asserts a realized situation in the actual world (past action completed), with no modal auxiliary, conditional, counterfactual, future, or optative context present.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have spoken' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by a past participle with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the set_to_0_if condition.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'have spoken' lacks the required passive morphology (auxiliary 'have' + 'been' + past participle); it is active voice with 'you' as the semantic agent performing the speaking action.

FullText: » I will show you the one to fear . Fear the one who , after he has killed , has power to destroy you throw into the ever-burning trash fires of the valley of Hinnom Greek : Gehenna . This is one you should fear .
ASTrns_explanation: The target verb 'has killed' introduces an overt grammatical subject (he) and an overt direct object (implied by the transitive verb 'kill' in context where the one being feared is the understood object), satisfying the criterion for ASTrns.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'killed' licenses an external argument (the implicit agent 'he' who performs the killing action), making it unergative/transitive rather than unaccusative.

DtRes_explanation: The verb 'had given thanks' is a communication/performative verb that reports a past interaction (the act of giving thanks) without entailing a persisting resulting state at the reference time; the thanks-giving event is completed but does not establish a binding directive or state that holds afterward.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'he had given thanks' describes a discrete, bounded event (the act of giving thanks) that occurred at a specific point in time, not a situation extending over a time span or holding continuously.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'he had given thanks' presents a completed transition event (giving thanks) as a bounded whole that precedes the next narrative step ('he said'), functioning as a discrete event in the narrative sequence.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had given thanks' is a past perfect form in a temporal clause ('when he had given thanks') that asserts a realized event in the actual world, with no modal, conditional, counterfactual, or hypothetical context present.

VcPass_explanation: The target 'had given thanks' is active voice (subject 'he' is the agent performing the action of giving), not passive voice; it lacks the required 'been' + past participle structure for VcPass.

FullText: » The Son of man indeed goes , as it has been determined . But woe to that man through whom he is betrayed ! «
ASTrns_explanation: The clause is morphologically passive ('has been determined'), and the lexical verb 'determine' licenses both an external argument (agent, unexpressed) and an internal argument (patient: the implicit subject 'it' referring to the Son of man's going), satisfying the passive construction rule.
ASUncc_explanation: The clause is morphologically passive ('has been determined'), and the lexical verb 'determine' licenses an external argument (an implicit agent responsible for the determining), which triggers the set_to_0_if condition for passive clauses.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'has been determined' is passive and licenses an internal argument (the implicit theme 'it' referring to the Son of man's going), thus failing the requirement for ASUnrg which demands no internal argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'has been determined' denotes a punctual transition into a decided state without a durative process phase; it marks a boundary moment of decision/establishment rather than a gradual change culminating in a result state.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'has been determined' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (a decision/decree being established) that can be modified by a specific time-point adverbial and does not permit interruption, satisfying the achievement criteria.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'has been determined' denotes a bounded perceptual/cognitive event marking the onset of a decision or decree with a defined result state (that something shall be thus), lacking dynamic durative unfolding and not naturally modifiable by 'for X time' adverbials.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has been determined' entails a transition into a resulting state (something was undetermined, now it is determined), which violates the semelfactive criterion of 'no resulting change of state'.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'has been determined' is morphologically marked as progressive perfect aspect ('has been'), which entails dynamic eventive unfolding and is explicitly incompatible with stative readings per the set_to_0_if rules.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'as it has been determined' presents a situation as actually occurring in the actual world (the Son of man goes as something has been determined), without any indication of counterfactuality, contrary-to-fact condition, or unfulfilled wish.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has been determined' is not negated; the negation 'woe' in the sentence applies to a different clause ('woe to that man through whom he is betrayed'), not to the target gram.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause contains no lexical past time adverb, phrasal past time adverb, or clausal past time anchor; the adverbial 'as' is a logical/causal connector, not a temporal one.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'has been determined' entails a change of state (a decision/plan now exists in determined form) that holds at the reference time, making it non-experiential rather than a life-history entry.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'has been determined' describes a change of state (a decision/determination has been made) and the resulting state (that it is determined/decided) is currently in effect at the reference time, as indicated by the present-tense context 'goes, as it has been determined.'
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'as it has been determined' describes a completed decision or transition into a determined state rather than a situation extending over time or holding continuously across a time span.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'as it has been determined' presents a bounded passive transition event (the act of determining/deciding) construed as a completed whole, matching the rule for 'have been' + past participle asserting a bounded event.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has been determined' asserts a realized state of affairs in the actual world (a past event with present relevance), with no modal auxiliary, conditional, counterfactual, future, or wish context present.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has been determined' includes 'been' followed by a past participle (not a progressive participle), which matches the set_to_1_if criterion for progressive-like constructions with 'been', indicating a passive perfect aspect rather than simple present perfect.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has been determined' instantiates the form auxiliary 'have' + 'been' + past participle of transitive verb 'determine', with grammatical subject 'it' corresponding to the semantic patient (the thing determined), satisfying the VcPass criteria.

FullText: » You have continued with me in my temptations .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'have continued' introduces an overt internal argument ('with me in my temptations'), satisfying the requirement for an overt grammatical object or complement that marks transitivity.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'continue' licenses an external argument (the subject 'You'), which is an agent/initiator performing the action of continuing with the speaker in temptations, thus failing the unaccusative criteria.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'have continued' licenses an internal argument ('with me in my temptations'), making it transitive and thus not an unergative verb.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'continue' denotes a durative state without an inherent endpoint or result state, naturally taking 'for X time' adverbials (e.g., 'continued for hours') rather than 'in X time', and lacks the bounded, goal-directed process structure required for AktAcc.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'have continued' denotes an ongoing durative process rather than a punctual transition into a new state; it unfolds over measurable duration and cannot be modified by a specific time-point adverbial in the same way achievements can.

AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have continued' entails an ongoing state or duration rather than a single punctual occurrence, and the perfect aspect indicates a resulting state of having remained in the temptations, which violates the semelfactive requirement of no resulting change of state.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'have continued' denotes dynamic progression and internal eventive unfolding over time, indicating ongoing change rather than a static state, which is incompatible with AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'You have continued with me in my temptations' presents a situation as having actually occurred in the actual world without any counterfactual marking, inverted conditional structure, or indication of unfulfilled wishes or contrary-to-fact conditions.

DtDTAM_explanation: The clause contains no lexical past time adverb, phrasal past time adverb, or clausal past time anchor; 'with me in my temptations' are prepositional phrases indicating accompaniment and location, not temporal reference.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'have continued' entails an ongoing or persisting state of accompaniment/support that holds at the reference time, which matches the set_to_0 criterion for verbs denoting a state that remains in effect.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'continue' describes an ongoing activity or state rather than a change of state or transition to a resulting state; it does not entail a new condition that holds at the reference time but rather the persistence of an existing situation.
DtUniv_explanation: The predicate 'have continued' denotes a non-terminating situation (remaining/staying with) that holds throughout the entire time span from past to present, satisfying the criterion for a situation extending to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'continued' denotes a stative situation of ongoing accompaniment/persistence without a construed boundary, and the clause does not present a transition event or bounded occurrence.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have continued' asserts a realized state of affairs in the actual world (present perfect indicating an action completed up to the present moment), with no modal auxiliary, conditional, counterfactual, future, or optative context present.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have continued' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by a past participle with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the set_to_0_if condition.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'have continued' lacks the required passive morphology (auxiliary 'have' + 'been' + past participle); 'continued' is a past participle in active voice with 'you' as the semantic agent performing the action, not the patient.

FullText: » I prayed for you that your faith would not fail . Once you have turned again strengthen your brothers . «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'have turned' introduces an overt internal argument ('again' functions as an adverbial modifier of the verb, but the construction 'turned again' is transitive in the sense that 'turn' here takes the reflexive/middle voice reading where the subject is both agent and patient; however, more directly, 'turn' in 'have turned again' can be read as introducing an implicit directional/locational complement, but the clearest reading is that this is a transitive use where 'you' (subject) and an implied object/complement are both present, satisfying the overt subject + internal argument requirement.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'turned' licenses an external argument (the implicit agent 'you' who performs the action of turning), and the clause contains a directional complement ('again') indicating directed motion/change with an agentive subject, not an unaccusative structure.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'turned' licenses an internal argument (the reflexive pronoun 'again' functions as a directional/locational complement indicating change of direction/state), and the construction denotes a change-of-state event where the subject undergoes a transformation rather than performing a volitional activity without an object.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'have turned' denotes a punctual boundary transition (change of direction or orientation) without a durative process phase, and does not naturally accept 'in X time' modification to indicate completion of a bounded event.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'have turned' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (turning away from faith failure back to faith), can be modified by a specific time-point adverbial ('once you have turned'), does not permit interruption as an ongoing process, and lacks measurable internal duration, satisfying multiple criteria for achievement aspect.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'have turned' denotes a punctual, bounded event with an inherent endpoint (the completion of turning), not a dynamic durative activity; it cannot be naturally modified by 'for X time' without implying completion, and lacks the homogeneous property required for activity classification.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have turned' entails a transition into a new resulting state (from not having turned to having turned), which disqualifies it from semelfactive status according to the set_to_0_if rule.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'have turned' denotes a bounded change-of-state event (turning back/repenting) with internal eventive unfolding and a clear completion boundary, not a static condition or mental state that holds over time.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'Once you have turned again' presents a future event as a real possibility within an open conditional structure ('Once you have turned...'), not as contrary to fact, and uses perfect aspect to mark temporal anteriority rather than counterfactuality.
ClNegPol_explanation: The negation 'not' in the sentence negates 'fail' (in 'would not fail'), not the target gram 'have turned', which appears in a separate clause without negation.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'Once you have turned again' contains 'Once', which is a conditional/exception marker establishing a hypothetical condition rather than a specific temporal anchor, and does not meet the criteria for lexical, phrasal, or clausal past time adverbials.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'have turned' entails a change of state/location (turning back to faith/allegiance) where the resulting state is relevant for the subsequent action ('strengthen your brothers'), making it non-experiential rather than a life-history entry.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'have turned' describes a change of state (turning back/repenting) where the resulting state of being turned/converted is entailed to hold at the reference time, enabling the subsequent action of strengthening brothers.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'Once you have turned again' describes a discrete bounded event (a transition into a new state of having turned/repented) rather than a situation holding continuously across a time span, and the temporal marker 'once' emphasizes the singular occurrence of this event.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'have turned' is a transition event (turning from one state to another) construed as a bounded, completed occurrence that serves as a discrete step in the narrative sequence ('Once you have turned again, strengthen your brothers'), presenting the situation as a single bounded event rather than ongoing or habitual.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have turned' appears in a temporal clause ('Once you have turned again') that asserts a realized condition as the prerequisite for the imperative action 'strengthen your brothers'; it is not marked as modal, conditional, future, or hypothetical, but rather presents the turning as a realized event that triggers the subsequent action.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have turned' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by a past participle with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the set_to_0_if condition.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'have turned' lacks the required passive morphology (auxiliary 'have' + 'been' + past participle); it is active voice with 'you' as the semantic agent performing the action of turning.

FullText: They responded : » What further need do we have for witnesses ? We have heard this from his mouth . «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'have heard' introduces an overt direct object 'this' (referring to testimony), satisfying the requirement for an overt internal argument.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'heard' licenses an external argument (the implicit agent 'we' who performed the hearing action) and introduces a direct object ('this'), making it transitive and not unaccusative.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'heard' licenses an internal argument (direct object 'this'), so it does not meet the criteria for ASUnrg which requires no internal argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'heard' denotes a punctual perceptual event—a near-instantaneous transition into awareness upon hearing speech—without a durative process phase leading to a defined endpoint, matching the achievement profile rather than accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'have heard' denotes a punctual perceptual transition into awareness (hearing/receiving information directly from the source), which matches the achievement criterion for cognitive/perceptual verbs marking instantaneous recognition of new information.

AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have heard' entails a transition into a resulting state of knowledge/perception and unfolds over a measurable duration (the act of hearing something requires time), rather than occurring as a single punctual occurrence.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'have heard' denotes a bounded perceptual event marking the completion of awareness (hearing something from his mouth), which encodes eventive change rather than a static condition, triggering the set_to_0_if rule for bounded perceptual events.

DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'We have heard this from his mouth' contains no lexical past time adverb, phrasal past time adverb, or clausal past time anchor; therefore DtDTAM is not instantiated.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'have heard' denotes perception where the focus is on the occurrence of the experience (hearing blasphemy directly from Jesus) at least once in the subject's history, rather than on a resulting state.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'have heard' is a perception event that does not entail a resulting state persisting at the reference time; it merely reports a past interaction without a persisting condition.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'We have heard this from his mouth' describes a discrete bounded event (a single past hearing) rather than a situation extending to the reference time or holding continuously across a time span.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'heard' denotes a stative situation (perception/knowledge state) without a construed boundary; the clause does not present a transition event or bounded occurrence but rather asserts an ongoing state of knowledge.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have heard' asserts a realized state of affairs in the actual world (they actually heard this from his mouth), with no modal, conditional, future, or wish context present.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have heard' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by a past participle with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the set_to_0_if condition.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'have heard' lacks the required passive morphology (auxiliary 'have' + 'been' + past participle); it is active voice with the grammatical subject 'We' as the semantic agent performing the action of hearing.

FullText: Herod was very happy to see Jesus . He had wanted to see him for a long time . He hoped to see him perform a miracle .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'had wanted' does not introduce an overt direct object; the infinitival complement 'to see him' is not a direct object argument but rather a non-finite clausal complement expressing the content of the wanting.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'wanted' licenses an external argument (the subject 'He' as agent/experiencer) and introduces an internal argument (the infinitival complement 'to see him'), making it unergative/transitive rather than unaccusative.

AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'wanted' denotes a punctual mental state transition (coming to desire) without a durative process phase leading to a defined endpoint; the 'for a long time' adverbial modifies the duration of the state itself, not a bounded accomplishment.

AktSemel_explanation: The event 'had wanted' unfolds over a measurable duration ('for a long time') rather than occurring as a single punctual occurrence, making it a durative state rather than a semelfactive event.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'had wanted' denotes a mental state (desire/wish) that persisted over time without internal dynamic development, matching the stative criterion of a cognitive condition holding over time.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'He had wanted to see him for a long time' uses past perfect to express temporal anteriority within a narrative (Herod's desire preceded the meeting), presenting a factual situation that actually occurred, not a counterfactual condition.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'had wanted' is not negated; there is no negator ('not', 'n't', 'never') or negative quantifier modifying it in the sentence 'He had wanted to see him for a long time.'
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'He had wanted to see him for a long time' contains 'for a long time', which is a vague durational phrase rather than a specific past time anchor (lexical, phrasal, or clausal), and therefore does not instantiate DtDTAM.
DtExp_explanation: The clause contains a duration adverbial ('for a long time') indicating a continuing situation rather than a completed life experience, which triggers the set_to_0_if condition.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'had wanted' is a stative verb describing a desire or mental state that existed in the past; it does not entail a change of state or a resulting state that holds at the reference time, and the duration adverbial 'for a long time' indicates a continuing situation rather than a completed transition.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'He had wanted to see him for a long time' contains the duration adverbial 'for a long time' and denotes a non-terminating state of wanting that extended continuously to the reference time, matching the positive example pattern.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'wanted' is a stative situation (desire/wish) without a construed boundary, and the duration adverbial 'for a long time' profiles the internal temporal span rather than packaging the situation as bounded.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had wanted' asserts a realized state of affairs in the past (Herod's desire existed as a fact), with no modal auxiliary, conditional, counterfactual, or future marking present.
TLTS_explanation: The auxiliary 'had' is morphologically past, making this a past perfect construction that instantiates TLTS.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had wanted' is active voice (auxiliary 'had' + past participle 'wanted' of a transitive verb), with the grammatical subject 'He' as the semantic agent (the one doing the wanting), not the patient; passive voice requires 'have/had' + 'been' + past participle.

FullText: He said : » You brought this man to me as one who perverted the people . I examined him before you and find no fault in this man . He has not done the things you accuse him of .
Target: has not done (the things you accuse him of)
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'done' introduces an overt internal argument ('the things you accuse him of'), satisfying the criterion for transitive verbs with direct objects.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'done' licenses an external argument (the implicit agent 'he') that performs the action of doing the things accused, satisfying the set_to_0_if condition for verbs licensing external arguments.

AktAch_explanation: The negated perfect 'has not done' denotes a state of non-occurrence over an extended period rather than a punctual transition into a new result state, and lacks the instantaneous boundary transition characteristic of achievements.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'done' with the internal argument 'the things you accuse him of' denotes a bounded, quantized situation with an inherent endpoint (completing the accused actions), which entails a specific result state rather than an ongoing dynamic activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The target 'has not done (the things you accuse him of)' refers to a complex set of actions with resulting states (perverting people), not a single punctual occurrence without change of state.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'has not done' denotes a bounded dynamic event (the performance or non-performance of actions) involving change-of-state and eventive unfolding, which triggers set_to_0_if criterion 3.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause presents the situation as factually occurring in the actual world (the man has not done these things), without any indication of counterfactuality, contrary-to-fact condition, or unfulfilled wish.
ClNegPol_explanation: The auxiliary 'has' is directly negated with 'not' in 'has not done', satisfying the first set_to_1_if condition.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'He has not done the things you accuse him of' contains no lexical past time adverb, phrasal past time adverb, or clausal past time anchor; therefore DtDTAM is not instantiated.
DtExp_explanation: The negative Perfect 'has not done' functions as a claim about the absence of the event in the subject's history, matching the CRI criterion for setting to 1.
DtRes_explanation: The clause is negated ('has not done'), which entails no transition to a resulting state; the CRI explicitly states to set to 0 when 'the clause negates the occurrence of the event; no transition to a resulting state is entailed.'
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes the absence of discrete bounded events (the accused things) rather than a situation holding continuously over time, and the negation focuses on non-occurrence of specific actions rather than duration.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause is negated with 'not' in a perfect construction asserting non-occurrence of an action, which leaves the situation open rather than presenting a bounded completion, and the stative reading of 'has not done' (denying an action) lacks a construed endpoint.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has not done' asserts a realized state of affairs in the actual world (the speaker's finding after examination), with negation alone not triggering irrealis per the edge case rule.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has not done' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by a past participle 'done', with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the set_to_0_if condition.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has not done' is active voice (auxiliary 'have' + past participle 'done' of a transitive verb), with the grammatical subject 'He' as the semantic agent and the object 'the things' as the semantic patient; it lacks the passive auxiliary sequence 'have' + 'been' + past participle required for VcPass.

ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'done' licenses an internal argument ('nothing'), which is a direct object, thus failing the requirement for ASUnrg.

DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'has done nothing worthy of death' describes the absence of a discrete bounded event (doing something worthy of death) rather than a situation holding continuously over time, and contains no duration adverbials or habitual markers indicating universal temporal extension.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'done nothing' is a stative situation asserting a negative state (absence of wrongdoing) without a construed boundary or transition, and the clause does not present a bounded occurrence but rather an ongoing condition.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has done nothing' asserts a realized state of affairs in the actual world (Herod's judgment about what has occurred), with no modal auxiliary, conditional, counterfactual, future, or optative context present.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has done nothing' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by past participle 'done' with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the set_to_0_if condition.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has done nothing' is active voice (auxiliary 'have' + past participle 'done' of a transitive verb), where the grammatical subject 'He' is the semantic agent (doer), not the patient; passive voice requires 'have' + 'been' + past participle.

FullText: A third time he said to them : » Why , what evil has this man done ? I have found no reason to put him to death . I will therefore chastise him and release him . «

AktAch_explanation: The verb 'has done (evil)' denotes a durative activity or process (committing evil acts) that unfolds over measurable duration rather than a punctual transition into a new state, and it lacks the instantaneous boundary quality required for achievement classification.

DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'My hour has not arrived' contains no lexical past time adverb, phrasal past time adverb, or clausal past time anchor; therefore DtDTAM is not instantiated.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'has not arrived' entails a change of location/state (the hour arriving/coming into being), and the resulting state (the hour not being present yet) holds at the reference time, which is a set_to_0_if condition.
DtRes_explanation: The clause is negated ('has not arrived'), so no transition to a resulting state is entailed; the negation prevents the motion verb from establishing that the mover has reached a destination and remains there.
DtUniv_explanation: The predicate 'has not arrived' denotes a transition into a new state (the arrival of the hour) and focuses on that transition rather than a situation holding continuously over time, matching the negative criterion for state-change verbs.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause is negated with 'not' (indicating the transition has not occurred at reference time), and the situation remains open rather than bounded.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has not arrived' asserts a realized state of affairs in the actual world (the hour has not yet come as a present fact), with negation alone not triggering irrealis per the edge case rule.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has not arrived' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by a past participle 'arrived' with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the set_to_0_if condition.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has not arrived' lacks the required passive morphology (auxiliary 'have' + 'been' + past participle); 'arrived' is an intransitive verb, and the construction functions as active voice with the subject as the semantic agent/experiencer, not a patient.

FullText: He said to him : » Every man sets out the good wine first . When the men have drunk freely the lower quality wine is served . You have kept the good wine until now . «
ASTrns_explanation: The target verb 'have drunk' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('the men') and an overt direct object ('freely' functions as an adverbial modifier, but the verb 'drunk' inherently takes wine/liquid as its internal argument, which is contextually established as 'the good wine' from the preceding clause, satisfying the transitive pattern requirement).
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'drink' licenses an external argument (the men as agent/experiencer who perform the action of drinking), so it does not meet the criteria for unaccusative verbs.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'have drunk' licenses an internal argument (the understood object 'wine'), satisfying the set_to_0_if condition that the target verb licenses or realizes an internal argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'have drunk' denotes a punctual or near-instantaneous change-of-state (transition into the state of intoxication) without a durative process phase; it cannot naturally be modified by 'in X time' to indicate completion of a bounded event with measured duration.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'have drunk' denotes a durative process of consuming alcohol over a measurable duration ('freely' indicates extended consumption), not a punctual transition into a new state, and can be naturally modified by 'finish' (e.g., 'when the men have finished drinking').
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'have drunk' denotes a completed action with an inherent endpoint (consumption of wine to satiation/completion), naturally modified by 'in X time' rather than 'for X time', and marks a bounded result state rather than an ongoing durative activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have drunk' entails a transition into a resulting state (men becoming intoxicated/satiated) and is construed as a durative activity rather than a single punctual occurrence.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'have drunk' denotes a dynamic activity involving consumption with internal eventive unfolding and change of state (from not having drunk to having drunk), which is incompatible with stative readings.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'When the men have drunk freely' presents a factual situation within a narrative description of actual practice, not a counterfactual condition or unfulfilled wish.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have drunk' is not negated; it appears in a conditional clause describing a positive eventuality, and no negator applies to it.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'When the men have drunk freely' contains the clausal past time anchor 'When', a temporal conjunction that establishes a specific temporal relationship.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'have drunk freely' describes a change of state (men becoming intoxicated) where the resulting state (being drunk) is relevant to the subsequent action (lower quality wine being served), making this narrative progression rather than experiential.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'have drunk' describes an activity (consumption) without entailing a persisting resulting state at the reference time; the clause merely reports a past event that triggers a subsequent action (serving lower quality wine) rather than a state that holds currently.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'When the men have drunk freely' describes a discrete bounded event (the completion of drinking) that triggers a subsequent action (serving lower quality wine), rather than a situation holding continuously across a time span.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'When the men have drunk freely' presents drinking as a bounded, completed transition event that serves as a discrete step in the narrative sequence ('When...then the lower quality wine is served'), packaging the situation as a bounded occurrence rather than ongoing.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have drunk' appears in a temporal clause ('When the men have drunk freely') that asserts a realized condition within the narrative world, not a modal, conditional, future, or hypothetical context.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have drunk' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by a past participle with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the set_to_0_if condition.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'have drunk' is active voice (subject 'men' is the semantic agent performing the action), not passive voice; passive voice requires 'have been' + past participle, not 'have' + past participle alone.

FullText: He said to him : » Every man sets out the good wine first . When the men have drunk freely the lower quality wine is served . You have kept the good wine until now . «
